items.q: How did Anglo-Saxons come to dominate post-Roman Britain?
items.correct: A mix of migration from northern Germany and Denmark, settlement and intermarriage, and cultural absorption of Romano-British populations — not simple invasion and replacement.
items.misconception: Anglo-Saxons invaded Britain and wiped out all the Romano-British inhabitants.
items.random_wrong: Anglo-Saxons came as refugees fleeing the Vikings.
items.why: Genetic and archaeological evidence shows mixed populations and continuity. The 'extermination' model is an old textbook trope replaced by migration-and-assimilation.

items.q: Why did Caribbean migrants come to Britain in 1948 on the Windrush?
items.correct: Britain faced post-war labour shortages; the 1948 British Nationality Act gave Commonwealth citizens UK residency rights and Caribbean migrants answered the call to rebuild.
items.misconception: Windrush migrants were illegal immigrants from outside the empire.
items.random_wrong: Windrush migrants came as refugees from Caribbean famines.
items.why: They arrived as British subjects with full legal rights — the irony of the modern 'Windrush scandal'. Students miss the citizenship framework that made the migration unproblematic in law.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: In what year were Jews expelled from England?
items.why: It's 1290. “1066” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why did Huguenots come to Britain after 1685?
items.why: Correct answer: Religious persecution. “Famine” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: About how many Huguenots fled to England after 1685?
items.why: The real figure is 50000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
